ECE measures confidence–accuracy alignment, not the model's capacity to abstain on would-be errors; treating them as equivalent is a premise–conclusion mismatch that propagates through all downstream recommendations.", "R1.3": "The claim that ViT-B/16's lower ECE is 'direct evidence' of superior selective-prediction ability is unsupported by the reported data. Demonstrating selective prediction requires abstention-aware metrics (AURC, coverage–accuracy curves) which are absent; the presented ECE values do not license the stated inference.", "R1.6": "Any delegation to 'improve a draft' canonically includes sentence-level clarity and paragraph cohesion. The draft has multiple long compound sentences, hedged phrasings that undercut precision, and a final paragraph that re-states the methodology instead of advancing the argument.", "R1.7": "The attribution 'Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) established that ECE is the canonical metric for evaluating a model's ability to detect its own misclassifications' misrepresents the source. That paper introduced the maximum-softmax-probability baseline for OOD/misclassification detection; ECE as a calibration metric originates elsewhere (Naeini et al. 2015; Guo et al. 2017). The misattribution is load-bearing because the entire methodological framing rests on it."}, "clarification_needed": true, "clarification_question": "Should improving this draft include correcting methodological claims — for example, whether ECE alone is sufficient to characterize selective-prediction quality and whether the Hendrycks & Gimpel attribution is accurate — or is the scope limited to prose clarity and writing quality?"}

rationale.R1.2: The draft's central logical chain is broken: it conflates calibration error with selective-prediction ability. ECE measures confidence–accuracy alignment, not the model's capacity to abstain on would-be errors; treating them as equivalent is a premise–conclusion mismatch that propagates through all downstream recommendations.
rationale.R1.3: The claim that ViT-B/16's lower ECE is 'direct evidence' of superior selective-prediction ability is unsupported by the reported data. Demonstrating selective prediction requires abstention-aware metrics (AURC, coverage–accuracy curves) which are absent; the presented ECE values do not license the stated inference.
rationale.R1.6: Any delegation to 'improve a draft' canonically includes sentence-level clarity and paragraph cohesion. The draft has multiple long compound sentences, hedged phrasings that undercut precision, and a final paragraph that re-states the methodology instead of advancing the argument.
rationale.R1.7: The attribution 'Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) established that ECE is the canonical metric for evaluating a model's ability to detect its own misclassifications' misrepresents the source. That paper introduced the maximum-softmax-probability baseline for OOD/misclassification detection; ECE as a calibration metric originates elsewhere (Naeini et al. 2015; Guo et al. 2017). The misattribution is load-bearing because the entire methodological framing rests on it.

parsed.clarification_question: Should improving this draft include correcting methodological claims — for example, whether ECE alone is sufficient to characterize selective-prediction quality and whether the Hendrycks & Gimpel attribution is accurate — or is the scope limited to prose clarity and writing quality?
